Retrieval augmented generation, often shortened to RAG, is a pattern where an application retrieves relevant text from a document collection before asking a language model to answer a user question. A basic pipeline usually has five stages. First, documents are uploaded and stored. Second, the system extracts clean text from each document. Third, the text is split into chunks so that each piece is small enough to embed and later place into an LLM prompt. Fourth, each chunk is turned into an embedding vector. Fifth, those vectors are stored in a vector database so that a query can retrieve the nearest chunks by semantic similarity instead of exact keyword matching.

Chunking matters because retrieval quality depends on how much context is preserved inside a chunk. If chunks are too small, important ideas get split apart and the search results lose meaning. If chunks are too large, the retrieved text may include too much irrelevant information and waste prompt space. A common compromise is to use chunk overlap. Overlap means the end of one chunk appears again at the start of the next chunk. This helps when a sentence about embeddings begins near one chunk boundary and finishes in the next chunk. Overlap is especially useful in retrieval augmented generation because the best answer often depends on a phrase that crosses a chunk boundary.

Embeddings are dense numeric vectors that capture semantic meaning. Two pieces of text that use different words can still end up near each other in vector space if they express similar ideas. For example, a user might ask about storing document vectors, while a document chunk explains how a vector database saves embeddings with payload metadata. Even if the wording does not match exactly, semantic search can still connect the question with the right chunk. This is the core improvement of retrieval augmented generation over simple substring search. Keyword search is still useful, but embeddings make it possible to retrieve meaning rather than only literal text.

The retrieved chunks are then inserted into the final prompt as supporting context. The language model should answer using those chunks and ideally cite the source document or filename. In a practical system, the answer should not come from the model alone. It should come from the combination of retrieval, ranking, and generation. That is why retrieval augmented generation is really two systems working together: an information retrieval layer and a language generation layer. If the retrieval layer returns poor context, the language model will still sound fluent, but the answer quality will be weak. Good RAG design therefore starts with strong chunking, high quality embeddings, and reliable semantic search.

When evaluating a RAG system, it helps to ask questions that should clearly map to one document and then questions that require semantic matching across several related chunks. You can ask what chunk overlap does, why embeddings are used, or how retrieval augmented generation differs from lexical search. If the vector database returns the correct chunk with a strong score, the retrieval stage is behaving well. If the answer later includes the right chunk text and source filename, the project is moving toward a complete hybrid RAG application.
